frameworks.name: evidence accumulation
frameworks.one_line: pulsatile sensory increments accumulated over time to drive perceptual choice

frameworks.name: gain modulation
frameworks.one_line: multiplicative scaling of sensory responses by cognitive and contextual variables

frameworks.name: top-down feedback
frameworks.one_line: non-sensory regions feed accumulated evidence and choice signals back to early visual cortex

core_question: Do early sensory cortical responses to discrete evidence pulses faithfully relay stimulus information, or are their amplitudes systematically shaped by accumulated evidence, choice, and other task contexts?
key_assumptions: cue-locked cells provide candidate momentary sensory inputs to an accumulator
per-cue response amplitudes act as multiplicative gains on a fixed impulse response
amplitude variability across cues can carry information beyond independent noise
manipulations: left vs right pulse sequences in a virtual T-maze accumulation task
recording site across six posterior cortical areas (V1, secondary visual areas, RSC) and layers 2/3 vs 5
head-fixation with theta-controlled vs free-viewing-angle trials
key_findings: only 5-15% of active neurons in V1 and secondary visual areas were cue-locked
cue-response amplitudes varied systematically with time-in-trial and behavioral context
about 50% of cue-locked cells in each area carried choice- or accumulated-evidence-dependent amplitude modulations
modulations were widespread across posterior cortex and persisted under view-angle control
interpretations: even earliest sensory representations of evidence pulses are gain-modulated by an accumulator and choice signal, suggesting top-down feedback from the decision process
behavioral models should treat sensory inputs as carrying temporally correlated noise rather than independent per-pulse noise
limitations_noted: cue-locked cells form only a small fraction of task-active neurons
limited statistical power per session for layer 5 in some areas
